pages.raw_text:
That the existing coal shortage
In this city is in part caused by
the Northern Pacific Railroad com.
pany confiscating coal offered for
shipment is emphatically denied
by General Agent Mitchell and
other N. P. officials in this city.


In reply the railroad officials
point to instances where in past
periods when fuel was scarce they
have turned over railroad coal for
commercial use.


They claim to have no record of
any commercial coal consigned to
this city being confiscated. While
the company, like all consumers at
this time, is economizing as much
as possible in the use of coal, 1t is
not yet reduced to the extremity
of appropriating that consigned to
local dealers, it is stated.


Cars are scarce at this time
largely on accountof heavy grain
shipmentsh, but N. P. officials be-
lieve they will be able to obtain
all needed to bring down what
coal the mines at Rock Springs
and in Wyoming can supply.


The whole trouble, it is explain
ed, is caused by the inability of the
mines to fill their orders. Labor
has been scarce This year and the
coal mines, according to railroad
information, have suffered particu-
larly in this respect.
